fields.heading: Introduction to Django
fields.content:
Django is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design. Built in 2003 and released in 2005, it follows the MVT (Model-View-Template) pattern.

Django's motto: 'The web framework for perfectionists with deadlines.'

Key Features:
- Built-in admin interface
- ORM — interact with DB using Python
- URL routing and template engine
- Built-in authentication system
- Security: CSRF, XSS, SQL injection protection
- Used by Instagram, Pinterest, NASA

fields.heading: MVT Architecture
fields.content:
Django uses MVT — Model, View, Template:

Model — Defines your data structure. Maps Python classes to database tables.

View — Business logic. Receives HTTP requests, talks to the model, returns responses.

Template — Presentation layer. HTML with Django Template Language for dynamic content.

URL Dispatcher — Routes incoming URLs to the correct view.

Flow: User Request → URL Dispatcher → View → Model → Template → Response

fields.heading: Prerequisites
fields.content:
Before installing Django, you need:

Python 3.8+ — download from python.org
pip — comes with Python 3.4+
Virtual Environment — strongly recommended

Why virtual environments? Each project may need different package versions. A venv keeps dependencies isolated so projects don't conflict.

fields.heading: Project Structure
fields.code:
mysite/
├── manage.py            # CLI tool for project management
├── mysite/              # Project config package
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── settings.py      # All project settings
│   ├── urls.py          # Root URL configuration
│   ├── asgi.py          # ASGI entry point
│   └── wsgi.py          # WSGI entry point
└── blog/                # An app you created
    ├── admin.py         # Register models with admin
    ├── apps.py          # App configuration
    ├── models.py        # Database models
    ├── views.py         # View functions/classes
    ├── urls.py          # App-level URL routes
    ├── tests.py         # Unit tests
    └── migrations/      # Auto-generated DB migrations

fields.heading: Understanding settings.py
fields.content:
The settings.py file is the control center of your Django project.

Key Settings:
- DEBUG — True in development, always False in production
- ALLOWED_HOSTS — Valid hostnames for your site
- INSTALLED_APPS — All apps Django should load
- DATABASES — Database connection config
- STATIC_URL — URL prefix for static files
- MEDIA_URL / MEDIA_ROOT — For user-uploaded files
- SECRET_KEY — Used for cryptographic signing — keep it secret!

fields.heading: Important settings.py Sections
fields.code:
SECRET_KEY = 'your-secret-key-here'
DEBUG = True
ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['localhost', '127.0.0.1']

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    'blog',           # Your custom app
]

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
        'NAME': BASE_DIR / 'db.sqlite3',
    }
}

STATIC_URL = '/static/'
STATICFILES_DIRS = [BASE_DIR / 'static']

MEDIA_URL = '/media/'
MEDIA_ROOT = BASE_DIR / 'media'

fields.heading: Django ORM & Models
fields.content:
A Model is a Python class that maps to a database table. Django's ORM lets you work with your database using Python instead of raw SQL.

Common Field Types:
- CharField — short text (max_length required)
- TextField — long text
- IntegerField — whole numbers
- BooleanField — True/False
- DateTimeField — date and time
- EmailField — validated email
- ImageField / FileField — file uploads
- ForeignKey — many-to-one relationship
- ManyToManyField — many-to-many
- OneToOneField — one-to-one

fields.heading: Defining Models
fields.code:
# blog/models.py
from django.db import models
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

class Category(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    slug = models.SlugField(unique=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.name

    class Meta:
        ordering = ['name']


class Post(models.Model):
    STATUS_CHOICES = [
        ('draft', 'Draft'),
        ('published', 'Published'),
    ]
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    slug = models.SlugField(unique=True)
    author = models.ForeignKey(
        User, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='posts'
    )
    category = models.ForeignKey(
        Category, on_delete=models.SET_NULL, null=True, blank=True
    )
    body = models.TextField()
    status = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=STATUS_CHOICES, default='draft')
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.title

    class Meta:
        ordering = ['-created_at']

fields.heading: Django ORM Queries
fields.code:
# python manage.py shell
from blog.models import Post

# CREATE
post = Post.objects.create(title='Hello', slug='hello', body='...')

# READ all
posts = Post.objects.all()

# FILTER
published = Post.objects.filter(status='published')

# GET single (raises exception if not found)
post = Post.objects.get(id=1)

# ORDER BY
latest = Post.objects.order_by('-created_at')

# LIMIT
top5 = Post.objects.all()[:5]

# UPDATE
Post.objects.filter(id=1).update(status='published')

# DELETE
Post.objects.filter(status='draft').delete()

# COUNT
total = Post.objects.count()

# SEARCH
results = Post.objects.filter(title__icontains='django')

# EXCLUDE
not_draft = Post.objects.exclude(status='draft')

fields.heading: Function-Based vs Class-Based Views
fields.content:
Views receive HTTP requests and return HTTP responses.

Function-Based Views (FBV) — Simple Python functions. Explicit, easy to read.

Class-Based Views (CBV) — Python classes. More reusable, less code for CRUD.

Built-in Generic CBVs:
- ListView — display a list of objects
- DetailView — display a single object
- CreateView — form to create an object
- UpdateView — form to update an object
- DeleteView — confirm and delete
- TemplateView — render a template
- RedirectView — redirect to a URL

fields.heading: Function-Based Views
Class-Based Views
URL Configuration
fields.content:
Django's URL dispatcher maps URL patterns to view functions.

URL Pattern Converters:
- <int:pk> — captures an integer
- <str:name> — captures a string
- <slug:slug> — captures a slug
- <uuid:id> — captures a UUID
- <path:subpath> — captures full path with slashes

Always name your URLs so you can reference them in templates and views without hardcoding paths.

fields.heading: URL Configuration Example
fields.code:
# mysite/urls.py (project-level)
from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('blog/', include('blog.urls')),
    path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),
]


# blog/urls.py (app-level)
from django.urls import path
from . import views

app_name = 'blog'

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.PostListView.as_view(), name='post_list'),
    path('<slug:slug>/', views.PostDetailView.as_view(), name='post_detail'),
    path('new/', views.PostCreateView.as_view(), name='create_post'),
    path('<slug:slug>/edit/', views.PostUpdateView.as_view(), name='update_post'),
    path('<slug:slug>/delete/', views.PostDeleteView.as_view(), name='delete_post'),
]

# In templates:
# {% url 'blog:post_list' %}
# {% url 'blog:post_detail' slug=post.slug %}

fields.heading: Django Template Language (DTL)
Base Template & Inheritance
Common Template Filters
Django Forms
fields.content:
Django forms handle HTML rendering, validation, and error display automatically.

Two types:
forms.Form — Standalone, not tied to a model. For contact, search, login forms.
forms.ModelForm — Auto-generates fields from a model. Perfect for create/update.

Form Workflow:
1. Define the form class
2. Instantiate in view (empty for GET, with data for POST)
3. Call form.is_valid() to validate
4. Access form.cleaned_data for safe values
5. Render with {{ form }} in template

fields.heading: Form & ModelForm
Form in Template
fields.code:
{% csrf_token %} {# Render all fields at once #} {{ form.as_p }} {# Or render field by field #}
{{ form.name.label_tag }} {{ form.name }} {% if form.name.errors %} {% for error in form.name.errors %}

{{ error }}

{% endfor %} {% endif %}

fields.heading: The Admin Interface
fields.content:
One of Django's most powerful features is the automatic admin interface. Register your models and get a full CRUD panel with zero extra code.

Features:
- List, create, edit, delete any registered model
- Search, filter, and sort records
- Inline editing of related models
- Bulk actions
- Permission-based access control

Access at: http://localhost:8000/admin/

Create superuser: python manage.py createsuperuser

fields.heading: Customising the Admin
Built-in Authentication
fields.content:
Django includes a complete authentication system:
- User login and logout
- Password hashing (PBKDF2 by default)
- Password reset via email
- User groups and permissions

User model fields: username, email, password, first_name, last_name, is_active, is_staff, is_superuser, date_joined

Protecting Views:
- @login_required — FBV decorator
- LoginRequiredMixin — CBV mixin
- request.user.is_authenticated — check in view

fields.heading: Auth Views & Decorators
Static vs Media Files
fields.content:
Static Files — CSS, JavaScript, images you include in your project.

Media Files — Files uploaded by users: profile photos, post images.

In Development: Django serves both when DEBUG=True.

In Production: Never use Django to serve static/media files. Use Nginx or a CDN (AWS S3). Run collectstatic to gather all files into one folder.

fields.heading: Configuration
fields.code: # settings.py STATIC_URL = '/static/' STATICFILES_DIRS = [BASE_DIR / 'static'] STATIC_ROOT = BASE_DIR / 'staticfiles' MEDIA_URL = '/media/' MEDIA_ROOT = BASE_DIR / 'media' # urls.py — serve media in development from django.conf import settings from django.conf.urls.static import static urlpatterns = [...] + static(settings.MEDIA_URL, document_root=settings.MEDIA_ROOT) # templates — using static files {% load static %} # Display user-uploaded image {% if post.image %} {% endif %}

fields.heading: Building APIs with DRF
fields.content:
Django REST Framework (DRF) is the standard library for Web APIs in Django.

It adds:
- Serializers — Convert model instances to/from JSON
- API Views — Function and class based API views
- ViewSets — Group related API views together
- Routers — Auto-generate API URLs from ViewSets
- Authentication — Token, Session, JWT
- Browsable API — Auto-generated HTML interface for testing

fields.heading: Serializers, ViewSets & Router
fields.code:
# blog/serializers.py
from rest_framework import serializers
from .models import Post

class PostSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    author_name = serializers.CharField(source='author.username', read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = Post
        fields = ['id', 'title', 'slug', 'body', 'status', 'author_name', 'created_at']
        read_only_fields = ['created_at']


# blog/views.py
from rest_framework import viewsets, permissions
from .serializers import PostSerializer

class PostViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Post.objects.filter(status='published')
    serializer_class = PostSerializer
    permission_classes = [permissions.IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly]

    def perform_create(self, serializer):
        serializer.save(author=self.request.user)


# blog/urls.py
from rest_framework.routers import DefaultRouter
router = DefaultRouter()
router.register('posts', PostViewSet, basename='post')

urlpatterns = [
    path('api/', include(router.urls)),
]
# Generates:
# GET/POST   /api/posts/
# GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE /api/posts/{id}/
